content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 20,
1993
Honorable John Burton
Assemblyman, District 12
455 Golden Gate Avenue
State Building,
Suite 2202
San Francisco,
CA
94102
RE:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93—162
Dear Assemblyman Burton:
You have requested advice regarding the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/
QUESTION
The state recently reimbursed you for a legislative trip
which was paid for out of your committee’s campaign bank account.
May you deposit the state’s reimbursement check into your personal
checking account and then use those funds to reimburse your
committee?
CONCLUSION
There is nothing in the Act which would prohibit you from
depositing the funds into your personal bank account and
reimbursing your campaign committee from your personal account.
ANALYSIS
The Act requires officeholders to disclose contributions
received and expenditures made for campaign purposes.
(Section
84200,
g.)
There is nothing in the Act that prohibits an
1/ Government Code Section 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Assemblyman John Burton
Our File No. A-93-162
Page
2
officeholder from reimbursing his/her campaign conunittee for
expenditures made by the committee as long as all of the
information required for disclosing the reimbursement and the
expenditure is properly reported.
Expenditures of $100 or more must be itemized on Schedule E
of the Form 490 disclosing the name and address of the payee,
a
description of the payment,
and the amount of the payment.
In
addition, whenever campaign funds are used to pay or reimburse the
travel expenses or necessary accommodations of a candidate/elected
official, his or her representative, or a member of the
candidate’s/elected official’s immediate family, the date of the
trip, the destination and total expenditure for each trip of $100
or more must also be reported.
The amount being reimbursed to the
committee must be reported as a miscellaneous increase to cash on
Schedule I of the Form 490.
(Section 84211.)
Although nothing in the Act would prohibit you from
depositing a state check into your personal account and then
reimbursing your committee for state expenses which were paid by
the committee,
Section 89510,
g., prohibit the personal use
of campaign funds.
Thus, the Act would not permit an officeholder
to retain for personal purposes funds received from a government
entity as reimbursement for expenses which were originally paid
out of the officeholder’s campaign account.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please contact me at 916/322-5662.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
Ann Kvasager’
Reform Consultant
By:

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The state recently reimbursed you for a legislative trip
which was paid for out of your committee’s campaign bank account.
May you deposit the state’s reimbursement check into your personal
checking account and then use those funds to reimburse your
committee?

CONCLUSION: There is nothing in the Act which would prohibit you from
depositing the funds into your personal bank account and
reimbursing your campaign committee from your personal account.
